title: Week 1 Recap
body:
Week 1 Recap – forward contracts

• Derivatives are financial assets whose value depend directly on some underlying variables (VIX and HDD) or assets (stocks and bonds).

• We focus on standard derivatives: forward, futures, swap and options.

• There are three types of investors: hedgers, speculators and arbitrageurs.

• We use the arbitrage pricing approach to determine derivative prices.

• Value of a forward contract: f0 = 0 and ft varies with spot price St and delivery price K at time 0 (or forward price at time 0, F0 ) • Long position: 𝒇𝒕 = 𝑺𝒕 - 𝑲𝒆^(−𝒓 𝑻 − 𝒕 ) • Short position: 𝒇𝒕 = 𝑲𝐞^(-r(T-t)) - 𝑺𝒕

• Forward price Ft is the delivery price when the contract is signed at time t

• At maturity T, the forward price and spot price must be equal 𝑺𝑻 =𝑭𝑻

Week 1 Recap - Example of a forward contract and its value

■ Party A is a gold miner, Party B is a gold purchaser. A and B agrees that A will sell to B 1.2 KG of the gold @ price of 24 USD/g in 187 days. ■ Gold currently is traded @ 23 USD/g

•A: Seller, short position At maturity: Sell the asset, get the cash for the price agreed on today (K) •B: buyer, long position At maturity: Buy the asset, pay the cash for the price agreed on today (K)

•K: 24 USD/g •T: 187 days, 187days - t0 •S0: 23 USD/g •f0: 0 (not always）

5 days later: ■ If S5=25 USD/g then for Party B: f5 = S5 - Ke^(-r*182/365) = 25 – 24e^(-10%*182/365) = 2.17 USD/g

At maturity: ■ If S65=50 USD/g then for Party B: f65 = S65 - K = 50 - 41 = 9 USD/g

Week 1 Recap - Futures contacts

• A futures contract is a standardized contract traded in exchanges

• Unlike forward, a vast majority of futures contracts are closed out prior to the delivery period.

• Futures contracts are settled daily • You pay (or receive) the change in settlement prices every day (as if you close out your position and re-open it daily), i.e., marking to market

• Margin account – initial margin and maintenance margin

• Together, margin requirements and daily settlements eliminate counterparty risk

• When the interest rate is deterministic, forward and futures prices are equal and denoted by F

Week 1 Recap - Example of Margins

Week 1 Recap - Futures v.s. Forwards

Week 1 Recap – volume, open interest and orders

• Volume is the number of contracts traded on the day

• Open interest is the number of live contracts (just long or just short position) at the end of previous day

• Trading volume can be greater than open interest. • Suppose A has 2 long positions and B has 2 short positions opened 2 days ago. • C opens 4 long positions & then closes them by shorting 4 contracts by trading with D. • At the end of the day, traders C and D have no outstanding positions. • On that day: Open interest = 2 (A & B) but Volume = 8 (C & D).

• When volume is much higher than open interest, this indicates day trading activity.

• Orders: market order, limit order, stop order, and stop-limit order.
